planting.note: Direct sow in fall to allow seeds to undergo natural cold stratification over winter; they will germinate the following spring when soil warms.

directions: Direct sow seeds in fall into well-drained, sandy or rocky soil in a full-sun location, pressing lightly into the surface without covering, and allow winter cold to provide the stratification seeds need to germinate the following spring. This robust southeastern native produces erect stems 2 to 4 feet tall crowned with large, dense magenta-purple flower heads in a distinctive bottom-up bloom sequence from August through October, with distinctly spreading phyllary bracts that give each head a scaly, star-like look unlike other blazing stars. Once established, it is highly drought-tolerant and requires no supplemental watering or fertilizing in lean, well-drained soil. It is an outstanding nectar source for monarchs, swallowtails, and native bees in late-summer and fall pollinator gardens throughout the Southeast.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center - Liatris squarrulosa

planting.note: Direct sow in fall into sandy, acidic soil in full sun; seeds require no cold stratification in Florida's mild winters and will germinate with seasonal rainfall.

directions: Direct sow seeds in fall into lean, dry, sandy, and acidic soil in full sun, pressing lightly onto the surface without burial; in Florida's mild climate, seeds germinate with cool-season rainfall and establish their corms over the first growing season. This Florida-endemic blazing star produces slender, wispy spikes of small rose-purple flower heads on thin, upright stems from late September through November, making it one of the latest-blooming native wildflowers available for Gulf Coast gardens. It is critically important as a late-season nectar source for migrating monarch butterflies and native bees when little else is in bloom. Plants are extremely drought-tolerant once established but will decline and rot in heavy clay or consistently wet soil.
sources: Florida Native Plant Society - Liatris laevigata

planting.note: Transplant container-grown plants into dry, sandy, acidic soil in spring after the last frost has passed and soil has begun to warm.

directions: Start seeds indoors in fall or winter in a sandy, acidic seed-starting mix and grow on in containers for transplanting the following spring into dry, lean, acidic sandy soil in full sun. This aromatic native perennial of longleaf pine savannas forms a striking basal rosette of broad, strap-like leaves that release a pleasant vanilla-like fragrance when dried, owing to the presence of coumarin, and historically the leaves were used as flavoring and fragrance. From August through October, slender, upright stems rise to 18-36 inches and bear flat-topped or rounded clusters of fluffy, lavender-pink composite flower heads that attract a wide variety of native bees, butterflies, and skippers. Once established in well-drained, acidic, sandy soil, it is reliably drought-tolerant and an excellent choice for longleaf pine savanna restoration or native pollinator gardens in the Southeast.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center - Carphephorus corymbosus

planting.note: Transplant into moist to wet, acidic, sandy or peaty soil in full sun after the last frost; do not allow the root zone to dry out during establishment.

directions: Start seeds indoors in a sandy, acidic mix in fall or winter and grow on for transplanting in spring into a moist to wet, sunny location with acidic, sandy or peaty soil that does not dry out in summer. Taller and more moisture-demanding than its relative Carphephorus corymbosus, this native perennial of wet longleaf pine savannas, flatwoods, and bog margins has hairy, upright stems, narrow rough leaves, and a loose, paniculate branching structure that produces fluffy, rose-purple composite flower clusters in late September and October. It is native to the coastal plain from North Carolina to Florida and Texas and provides critically late-season nectar for monarch butterflies, native bees, and skippers in Gulf Coast gardens. Best performance comes from consistently moist to wet, acidic soil in full sun; plants will struggle in dry or alkaline conditions.
sources: Florida Native Plant Society - Carphephorus paniculatus

planting.note: Direct sow in fall into sandy or average, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; seeds overwinter and germinate the following spring.

directions: Direct sow seeds in fall onto the surface of sandy or average, well-drained soil in a partially shaded to fully sunny spot, allowing winter cold to promote germination the following spring. This distinctive southeastern native forms a bold basal rosette of large, rough, spatula-shaped leaves from which wiry, branching stems rise 12-24 inches and bear small, fuzzy, lilac-pink composite flower heads in late summer and fall, providing nectar for native bees and small butterflies at a time when many other plants have finished blooming. It differs from the closely related Elephantopus tomentosus in having noticeably smoother leaves and stems. Plants adapt well to open woodland gardens, partial-shade borders, and disturbed natural areas with average to moderate soil moisture, and they naturalize steadily from self-sown seed without becoming invasive.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center - Elephantopus nudatus
